summary: Gruber responds to Chuq Von Rospach's review of Apple's 2016, focusing on two neglected product lines: AirPort routers and the Mac Pro. He argues that Apple's AirPort lineup has no defensible reason to exist in its current outdated state — it should either be updated or killed. On the Mac Pro, Gruber contends that the 2013 'trash can' design was built to prove critics wrong rather than serve power users, and that selling 1000-day-old workstations at original prices is an unmitigated disaster. He notes that Apple's pricing behavior suggests they still plan to replace the Mac Pro, but have failed to execute for unknown reasons.
key_points: Apple's AirPort lineup has been left to languish while mesh networking emerged, with no reasonable justification for keeping outdated products on sale
The 2013 Mac Pro was designed around Apple's ego needs to counter 'can't innovate' criticism rather than serving what power users actually needed
The Mac Pro's non-configurable, non-upgradeable design isn't necessarily unsound, but the failure to update it annually made it a disaster

Apple's pricing strategy provides a signal: unchanged Mac Pro prices suggest Apple intends to replace the product rather than abandon it, since price drops without replacements would signal exit from the market
Multiple reasonable paths existed — updates, redesign, or discontinuation — but Apple chose the one path that makes no sense: stasis
money_quotes: There's no reasonable argument for the current AirPort state of affairs.
"What the hell happened with the Mac Pro?" is the most interesting question about Apple today.
What they built was a device based around their own ego needs of proving their critics wrong, not a device that served the purposes of their power users.
If Apple had updated the Mac Pro on a roughly annual basis, we wouldn't be calling this a disaster.
Selling 1000-day-old pro workstations at the same prices as in 2013 makes no sense.
Whatever the explanation is, this situation is an unmitigated disaster.
Updates to the same basic design would make sense. An all-new design would make sense. Getting out of the Mac Pro game would make sense.
key_insight: Apple's failure with the Mac Pro and AirPort isn't about making wrong strategic choices — it's about making no choice at all, leaving products in an indefensible limbo that serves no one.
